claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let me begin by asking you some questions about your child.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

Child Benefit eligibility depends on the age of the child. Could you tell me:
- **How old is your child?**

This will help me determine which eligibility path applies to your situation:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
- Young people aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those aged 20 or over generally don't qualify

Please let me know your child's age, and we'll continue from there.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're asking about your son Alex who is 8 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, he falls into the automatic qualification age category. Let me move to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old, I assume he's not working at all, but I need to confirm this to continue with the assessment. This requirement ensures that children aren't working full-time while you're claiming Child Benefit for them.

Could you confirm that Alex is either not working or working less than 24 hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at all - he's just a kid in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's what I expected! Since Alex is in primary school and not working, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, I need to confirm that Alex is not receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name. 

Since Alex is 8 years old and in primary school, I imagine he's not claiming any benefits in his own right, but I need to confirm this for the assessment. Can you confirm that's correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he doesn't get any benefits in his own name - he's only 8!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes sense! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?**

Again, since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, I expect he's not in any apprenticeship program at all. Apprenticeships are typically for young people aged 16 and over. 

Can you confirm Alex is not in any apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's only 8 and still in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now we're moving on to check about **your** circumstances. This question is about where you live.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You can meet this requirement in several ways. Do any of the following apply to you?

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient financial resources (not claiming certain benefits)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with the right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Could you tell me about your residency situation?** Do you normally live in the UK, or does one of the other circumstances apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you normally live in the UK (in Manchester), you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about your responsibility for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can meet this requirement in several ways:

1. **You live with Alex** (this is the most common situation)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep by paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be (or equivalent in kind) towards his care - this includes things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds the Child Benefit payment

**Do you live with Alex, or do you contribute to his upkeep in another way?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me - he lives with his mum.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Since Alex doesn't live with you, let me ask about the other ways you might be responsible for him.

**Do you contribute to Alex's upkeep financially?**

This could include:
- Paying maintenance under a court order or agreement
- Contributing money for his care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing)
- Making payments or contributions that equal or exceed the Child Benefit amount (which would be around £25 per week for the eldest/only child, or about £16.50 per week for additional children)

The contributions can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments, and can also be "in kind" (like buying clothes, food, or presents).

**Do you make regular financial contributions towards Alex's care that would be at least equivalent to the Child Benefit amount?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not contributing financially at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for being honest about your situation. Based on what you've told me, let me move to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Result: Not Eligible

I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**.

### Why You're Not Eligible

While Alex himself meets all the requirements as a qualifying child (he's 8 years old, in school, not working, and not receiving benefits), **you don't meet the responsibility requirement** for claiming Child Benefit.

To claim Child Benefit, you must be responsible for the child, which means **either**:
- Living with the child, **OR**
- Contributing financially to their upkeep by paying at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their care (this includes things like food, clothes, housing, pocket money, maintenance payments, etc.)

Since Alex lives with his mother and you're not currently contributing financially to his care, you don't meet this requirement.

### What This Means

**Alex's mother would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit** for him, as she lives with him and is responsible for his day-to-day care.

### If Your Situation Changes

If you begin making regular maintenance payments or other financial contributions towards Alex's care that equal or exceed the Child Benefit amount (currently around £25 per week for an eldest/only child), you may become eligible to claim. However, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time.

If you have any questions or if your circumstances change, you can find more information at: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which means he falls under the automatic age qualification (under 16). He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own name, and is not in an apprenticeship, which are all positive factors. However, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement. Alex lives with his mother, and the claimant is not contributing financially to Alex's upkeep. To claim Child Benefit, you must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them OR contributing at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their care. Since neither condition is met, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is asking about eligibility for their 8-year-old son Alex. While Alex meets all the child-specific qualifying criteria (age, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship) and the claimant meets the UK residency requirement, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement as they neither live with Alex nor contribute financially to his upkeep. Alex lives with his mother, who would be the appropriate person to claim Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries
